text.title: Cognitive Inclusion Design Rules and Agent Limitations
text.summary: Testable rules for implementing cognitive inclusion, with evidence caveats and known agent failure modes.

rule.statement: Make all cognitive customization settings (font, spacing, motion, focus mode) discoverable, persistent across sessions, and accessible without navigating into deep settings menus.
rule.rationale: A customization option that users cannot find or that resets on each visit provides zero benefit; discoverability and persistence are the minimum viability bar.

rule.statement: Do not remove motion globally based on prefers-reduced-motion alone; audit which animations are critical (focus indicators, progress feedback) before suppressing them.
rule.rationale: Indiscriminate motion removal breaks critical feedback signals that sighted users rely on for orientation; targeted suppression preserves function.

rule.statement: Review time-limit removal with the security team; session-timeout limits may be non-negotiable security requirements that need a joint UX + security solution.
rule.rationale: Time limits often exist for security reasons; unilateral removal creates vulnerabilities that require a coordinated design rather than a UX-only decision.

antipattern.description: Applying a dyslexia font (e.g., OpenDyslexic) as the global default for all users.
antipattern.reason: Empirical evidence for dyslexia fonts as universal defaults is weak; offer as a user-controlled option rather than imposing on users who may find it harder to read.

antipattern.description: Using plain-language rewriting as a substitute for plain-language editing of the original content.
antipattern.reason: Cognitive design patterns assume content is already written in plain language; restructuring a screen cannot fix fundamentally complex or jargon-heavy source text.

antipattern.description: Agent runs automated tools only and declares cognitive accessibility addressed.
antipattern.reason: Automated tools cannot detect cognitive issues; structured manual review plus user research with cognitively diverse participants is required.

antipattern.description: Agent rewrites branded copy to plain language without coordinating with the tone-of-voice guidelines.
antipattern.reason: Plain-language rewriting can conflict with brand voice; an exception list for branded terms and voice must be agreed before bulk rewrites.
